documentation:

```
param price

the Black-Scholes option price

underlying asset price

float

strike price

float

param t

time to expiration in years

type t

float

param r

risk-free interest rate

type r

float

param flag

'c' or 'p' for call or put.

type flag

str
```

```pycon
>>> S = 100
>>> K = 100
>>> sigma = .2
>>> r = .01
>>> flag = 'c'
>>> t = .5
```

```pycon
>>> price = black_scholes(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> iv = implied_volatility(price, S, K, t, r, flag)
```

```pycon
>>> expected_price = 5.87602423383
>>> expected_iv = 0.2
```

```pycon
>>> abs(expected_price - price) < 0.00001
True
>>> abs(expected_iv - iv) < 0.00001

```

functions.name: py_vollib.black_scholes.greeks.analytical.theta
documentation:
Return Black-Scholes theta of an option.

```
param S

underlying asset price

type S

float

param K

strike price

type K

float

param sigma

annualized standard deviation, or volatility

type sigma

float

param t

time to expiration in years

type t

float

param r

risk-free interest rate

type r

float

param flag

'c' or 'p' for call or put.

type flag

str
```

The text book analytical formula does not divide by 365,
but in practice theta is defined as the change in price
for each day change in t, hence we divide by 365.

Example 17.2, page 359, Hull:

```pycon
>>> S = 49
>>> K = 50
>>> r = .05
>>> t = 0.3846
>>> sigma = 0.2
>>> flag = 'c'
>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma) * 365
>>> # -4.30538996455
>>> annual_theta_text_book = -4.31
>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_text_book) < .01
True
```

Using the same inputs with a put.
\>>> S = 49
\>>> K = 50
\>>> r = .05
\>>> t = 0.3846
\>>> sigma = 0.2
\>>> flag = 'p'
\>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma) * 365
\>>> # -1.8530056722
\>>> annual_theta_reference = -1.8530056722
\>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_reference) < .000001
True

functions.name: py_vollib.black_scholes.greeks.analytical.vega
documentation:
Return Black-Scholes vega of an option.

```
param S

underlying asset price

type S

float

param K

strike price

type K

float

param sigma

annualized standard deviation, or volatility

type sigma

float

param t

time to expiration in years

type t

float

param r

risk-free interest rate

type r

float

param flag

'c' or 'p' for call or put.

type flag

str
```

The text book analytical formula does not multiply by .01,
but in practice vega is defined as the change in price
for each 1 percent change in IV, hence we multiply by 0.01.

Example 17.6, page 367, Hull:

```pycon
>>> S = 49
>>> K = 50
>>> r = .05
>>> t = 0.3846
>>> sigma = 0.2
>>> flag = 'c'
>>> vega_calc = vega(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> # 0.121052427542
>>> vega_text_book = 0.121
>>> abs(vega_calc - vega_text_book) < .01
True
```

functions.name: py_vollib.black_scholes.greeks.numerical.test
documentation:
Test by comparing analytical and numerical values.

```pycon
>>> flag='c'
>>> S=1000.0
>>> K=1000.0
>>> t=0.1
>>> r=0.05
>>> sigma=0.3
```

```pycon
>>> epsilon = 0.01
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = delta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> v2 = adelta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = gamma(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> v2 = agamma(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = rho(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> v2 = arho(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = vega(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> v2 = avega(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> v2 = atheta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```


```pycon
>>> v1 = delta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma)
```

functions.name: py_vollib.black.normalised_black
documentation:
Calculate the normalised Black value,
a "time value put-call invariant" transformation
of the Black pricing formula. In other words,
the amount of time value, or "extrinsic" value
of a put and call at the same log-moneyness will
be always be identical.

```
param x

ln(F/K) where K is the strike price, and F is the futures price

type x

float

param s

volatility times the square root of time to expiration

type s

float

param flag

'p' or 'c' for put or call

type flag

str
```

```pycon
>>> def normalised_intrinsic(F, K, flag):
...     if flag=='c':
...         return max(F-K,0)/(F*K)**0.5
...     else:
...         return max(K-F,0)/(F*K)**0.5
```

```pycon
>>> F = 100.
>>> K = 95.
>>> x = log(F/K)
>>> t = 0.5
>>> v = 0.3
>>> s = v * sqrt(t)
```

```pycon
>>> normalised_black(x,s,'p')
0.061296663817558904
```

```pycon
>>> normalised_black(x,s,'c')
0.11259558142181655
```

'''
Here the put is OTM, so has only time value.
The call is ITM, having both intrinsic and time value.
Since the time value must be equal for both,
the call normalised price minus its normalised
intrinsic must equal the put normalised price.

```pycon
>>> (normalised_black(x,s,'p') - (
... normalised_black(x,s,'c') - normalised_intrinsic(F,K,'c')))<1e-12
True
```

functions.name: py_vollib.ref_python.black_scholes.greeks.analytical.theta
documentation:
Return Black-Scholes theta of an option.

```
param S

underlying asset price

type S

param K

strike price

float

param sigma

annualized standard deviation, or volatility

type sigma

float

param t

time to expiration in years

type t

float

param r

risk-free interest rate

type r

float

param flag

'c' or 'p' for call or put.

type flag

str
```

The text book analytical formula does not divide by 365,
but in practice theta is defined as the change in price
for each day change in t, hence we divide by 365.

Example 17.2, page 359, Hull:

```pycon
>>> S = 49
>>> K = 50
>>> r = .05
>>> t = 0.3846
>>> sigma = 0.2
>>> flag = 'c'
>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma) * 365
>>> # -4.30538996455
>>> annual_theta_text_book = -4.31
>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_text_book) < .01
True
```

Using the same inputs with a put.
\>>> S = 49
\>>> K = 50
\>>> r = .05
\>>> t = 0.3846
\>>> sigma = 0.2
\>>> flag = 'p'
\>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma) * 365
\>>> # -1.8530056722
\>>> annual_theta_reference = -1.8530056722
\>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_reference) < .000001
True

```pycon
>>> S =  49
```

```pycon
True
```

```pycon
True
```

```pycon
True
```

```pycon
True
```

functions.name: py_vollib.ref_python.black_scholes_merton.greeks.analytical.theta
documentation:
Returns the Black-Scholes-Merton theta of an option.

```
param flag

'c' or 'p' for call or put.

type flag

str

param S

underlying asset price

type S

float

param K

strike price

type K

float

param t

time to expiration in years

type t

float

param r

annual risk-free interest rate

type r

float

param sigma

volatility

type sigma

float

param q

annualized continuous dividend yield

type q

float

returns

float
```

The text book analytical formula does not divide by 365,
but in practice theta is defined as the change in price
for each day change in t, hence we divide by 365.

Example 17.2, page 359, Hull:

```pycon
>>> S = 49
>>> K = 50
>>> r = .05
>>> t = 0.3846
>>> q = 0
>>> sigma = 0.2
>>> flag = 'c'
>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q) * 365
>>> # -4.30538996455
>>> annual_theta_text_book = -4.31
>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_text_book) < .01
True
```

Using the same inputs with a put.
\>>> S = 49
\>>> K = 50
\>>> r = .05
\>>> t = 0.3846
\>>> sigma = 0.2
\>>> flag = 'p'
\>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q) * 365
\>>> # -1.8530056722
\>>> annual_theta_reference = -1.8530056722
\>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_reference) < .000001
True

functions.name: py_vollib.black_scholes_merton.greeks.numerical.test_analytical_vs_numerical
documentation:
Test by comparing analytical and numerical values.

```pycon
>>> flag='c'
>>> S=1000.0
>>> K=1000.0
>>> t=0.1
>>> r=0.05
>>> sigma=0.3
>>> q = 0.05
>>> sigma = 0.2
>>> flag = 'c'
```

```pycon
>>> epsilon = 0.01
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = delta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)
>>> v2 = adelta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = gamma(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)
>>> v2 = agamma(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = rho(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)
>>> v2 = arho(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)
>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = vega(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)

True
```

```pycon
>>> v1 = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q)

>>> abs(v1-v2)<epsilon
True
```

functions.name: py_vollib.black_scholes_merton.greeks.analytical.theta
documentation:
Returns the Black-Scholes-Merton theta of an option.

```
param flag

'c' or 'p' for call or put.

type flag

str

param S

underlying asset price

type S

float

param K

strike price

type K

float

param t

time to expiration in years

type t

float

param r

annual risk-free interest rate

type r

float

param sigma

volatility

type sigma

float

param q

annualized continuous dividend yield

type q

float

returns

float
```

The text book analytical formula does not divide by 365,
but in practice theta is defined as the change in price
for each day change in t, hence we divide by 365.

Example 17.2, page 359, Hull:

```pycon
>>> S = 49
>>> K = 50
>>> r = .05
>>> t = 0.3846
>>> q = 0
>>> sigma = 0.2
>>> flag = 'c'
>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q) * 365
>>> # -4.30538996455
>>> annual_theta_text_book = -4.31
>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_text_book) < .01
True
```

Using the same inputs with a put.
\>>> S = 49
\>>> K = 50
\>>> r = .05
\>>> t = 0.3846
\>>> sigma = 0.2
\>>> flag = 'p'
\>>> annual_theta_calc = theta(flag, S, K, t, r, sigma, q) * 365
\>>> # -1.8530056722
\>>> annual_theta_reference = -1.8530056722
\>>> abs(annual_theta_calc - annual_theta_reference) < .000001
True

Note about the parameter "b":
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

::

======================================================================================
from Espen Gaarder Haug's
"The Complete Guide to Option Pricing Formulas," Second Edition,
page 90.

+-----------+------------------------------------------------------+
| b = r     |  gives the Black and Scholes (1973) stock option     |
|           |  model                                               |
+-----------+------------------------------------------------------+
| b = r -q  |  gives the Merton (1973) stock option model with     |
|           |  continuous dividend yield q                         |
